lines.text: Introduction, part two. The game theory approach.
At the center of all this is one idea: equilibrium. People also call it G T O, for game theory optimal.
An equilibrium strategy has a remarkable property. It's a way of playing that can't be beaten, even by an opponent who knows exactly what you're doing.
How is that possible? The secret is that you think in ranges, not single hands. You build betting ranges that mix strong hands and bluffs in the right proportions.

Get those proportions right, and your opponent is stuck. If they fold too much, your bluffs print money. If they call too much, your value bets clean them out. Every choice they make is break-even at best.
That balance point, where the opponent literally cannot find a profitable response, is the equilibrium. It's your safety net. It guarantees you can't be exploited.
Now, equilibrium is not the same as maximally profitable. Against a weak opponent, you can often win more by deviating, by exploiting their specific leaks.
But here's why you start with the baseline. You can't recognize a leak, or exploit it safely, until you know what solid, balanced play looks like in the first place.
So the plan is simple to state, even if it takes a book to master. Learn the unexploitable baseline. Then learn how, and when, to step away from it to punish the mistakes in front of you.
